name: Mathematics
description: From pre-algebra foundations through calculus and linear algebra. Rebuild your math intuition from the ground up.

nodes.name: Polynomials
nodes.description: From linear to quadratic to higher degrees. Master the building blocks of algebra.
nodes.whyItMatters: Parabolas model projectile motion, profit optimization, and signal processing. The quadratic formula is one of the most useful tools in applied math.
practiceProblems: Factor: x² + 5x + 6
Solve using formula: 2x² - 4x - 6 = 0
Find vertex of: y = x² - 6x + 5
Complete the square: x² + 8x + __

resources.title: Khan Academy: Quadratics
resources.whyHelpful: Covers factoring, quadratic formula, and completing the square systematically. Use this as the primary resource for polynomials.

resources.title: Quadratic Formula Derivation
resources.whyHelpful: Derives the formula from first principles. Understanding WHY it works makes it easier to remember and apply.

resources.title: Quadratic Visualizer (Desmos)
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive graphing lets you see how coefficients a, b, c shift the parabola. Drag sliders to build real intuition.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Polynomials
resources.whyHelpful: Broader coverage beyond quadratics — polynomial arithmetic, division, and graphs. Essential for later algebra topics.

workedExamples.problem: Factor x² - 5x + 6. Then solve 2x² - 8x - 10 = 0 using the quadratic formula.
workedExamples.explanation: Factoring finds two numbers that multiply to c and add to b. The quadratic formula always works, even when factoring is difficult. The discriminant (b² - 4ac) tells you how many real solutions exist.

workedExamples.problem: Complete the square for x² + 6x + 2 = 0. Then find the vertex and axis of symmetry.
workedExamples.solution: x² + 6x + 2 = 0 → x² + 6x = -2 → x² + 6x + 9 = -2 + 9 → (x + 3)² = 7 → x = -3 ± √7. Vertex form: y = (x + 3)² - 7. Vertex: (-3, -7). Axis of symmetry: x = -3.
workedExamples.explanation: Completing the square converts ax² + bx + c to a(x - h)² + k form, revealing the vertex (h, k). Take half of b, square it, add to both sides. This technique is essential for understanding the shape and position of parabolas.

workedExamples.problem: A ball is thrown upward with height h(t) = -5t² + 20t + 1. Find the maximum height and when it occurs. When does the ball hit the ground?
workedExamples.explanation: Quadratic functions model parabolic motion. The vertex gives the maximum (for downward-opening parabolas) or minimum. The roots tell you when the object is at ground level. Discard negative time values as physically meaningless.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to set the equation to zero before factoring — 2x² - 8x = 10 must become 2x² - 8x - 10 = 0 first.
Sign errors in the quadratic formula — -b becomes +8 when b = -8. Be careful with negatives.
Stopping at factoring without solving — (x-2)(x-3) = 0 means x = 2 or x = 3, not just the factors.

quiz.question: Factor: x² + 7x + 12
quiz.explanation: We need two numbers that multiply to 12 and add to 7: 3 and 4. So x² + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4).

quiz.question: What does the discriminant b² - 4ac tell you?
options: The number and type of solutions
quiz.explanation: If discriminant > 0: two real solutions. = 0: one real solution. < 0: no real solutions (two complex).

quiz.question: What is the vertex of y = x² - 6x + 5?
quiz.explanation: Vertex x-coordinate: -b/(2a) = 6/2 = 3. y = 9 - 18 + 5 = -4. So vertex is (3, -4).

quiz.question: What is the derivative of f(x) = 3x⁴ - 2x² + 5x - 7?
quiz.explanation: Using the power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹. So: 3·4x³ - 2·2x + 5·1 - 0 = 12x³ - 4x + 5.

quiz.question: If x² + bx + 9 is a perfect square trinomial, what is b?
quiz.explanation: Perfect square: x² + bx + 9 = (x ± 3)² = x² ± 6x + 9. So b = ±6. Both 6 and -6 work.

nodes.name: Functions
nodes.description: The concept that changed math forever. Input → output, with graphs and transformations.
nodes.whyItMatters: Functions are the building blocks of calculus, programming, and machine learning. Every model is a function that maps inputs to outputs.
practiceProblems: If f(x) = 2x + 1, find f(3) and f(-2)
Find domain of f(x) = √(x - 4)
Find inverse of f(x) = 3x - 2
Compute f(g(2)) where f(x)=x², g(x)=x+1

resources.title: Khan Academy: Functions
resources.whyHelpful: Systematic introduction to function notation, domain, range, and transformations. The most structured course for this topic.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Domain & Range
resources.whyHelpful: Focuses specifically on domain/restrictions — the hardest part of functions. Critical for avoiding runtime errors in programming.

resources.title: Function Machine (Mathsisfun)
resources.whyHelpful: Simple visual explanation with interactive examples. Good for when the Khan videos feel too fast.

resources.title: BetterExplained: Functions
resources.whyHelpful: Intuitive explanations that focus on WHY. Read this after Khan to deepen understanding.

workedExamples.problem: If f(x) = x² - 3x and g(x) = 2x + 1, find f(g(2)) and g(f(2)). Are they equal?
workedExamples.solution: g(2) = 2(2) + 1 = 5. f(g(2)) = f(5) = 25 - 15 = 10. f(2) = 4 - 6 = -2. g(f(2)) = g(-2) = -4 + 1 = -3. They are not equal — function composition is not commutative.
workedExamples.explanation: f(g(x)) means apply g first, then f. g(f(x)) means apply f first, then g. In general, f(g(x)) ≠ g(f(x)). Order matters in composition.

workedExamples.problem: Find the inverse of f(x) = (2x + 5)/3. Verify that f(f⁻¹(x)) = x and f⁻¹(f(x)) = x.
workedExamples.explanation: To find an inverse: (1) write y = f(x), (2) swap x and y, (3) solve for y. The inverse 'undoes' the original function. Verifying f(f⁻¹(x)) = x confirms the inverse is correct. Not all functions have inverses — they must be one-to-one.

workedExamples.problem: Given f(x) = √(x - 1) and g(x) = x² + 1, find the domain of f, the range of f, and compute f(g(3)) and g(f(3)). Are f and g inverses?
workedExamples.solution: Domain of f: x - 1 ≥ 0, so x ≥ 1. Range of f: √(non-negative) ≥ 0, so range is [0, ∞). f(g(3)) = f(9+1) = f(10) = √9 = 3. g(f(3)) = g(√2) = 2 + 1 = 3. Interesting: f(g(x)) = √(x²+1-1) = √(x²) = |x|. But g(f(x)) = (√(x-1))² + 1 = x - 1 + 1 = x (for x ≥ 1). They are NOT inverses of each other because f(g(2)) = |2| = 2 works, but f(g(-2)) = |-2| = 2 ≠ -2.
workedExamples.explanation: Two functions are inverses only if f(g(x)) = x AND g(f(x)) = x for ALL x in both domains. Here, f(g(x)) = |x| ≠ x for negative x, so they are NOT inverses. The absolute value is the key trap — √(x²) = |x|, not x.

workedExamples.problem: A company models its profit as a function: P(n) = -0.5n² + 20n - 80, where n is the number of units sold. At what unit price n does the company break even (P(n) = 0)? What is the maximum profit?
workedExamples.explanation: Quadratic functions (ax² + bx + c) model real-world optimization problems. The vertex gives the max/min. zeros (from the quadratic formula) give break-even points. Always check if the solutions make sense — a business can't sell a fraction of a unit.

workedExamples.problem: For a given profit function P(x) = -2x² + 12x - 10, what is the vertex (profit-maximizing units)? Verify your answer by computing P(3) and P(4) to confirm it's the maximum.
workedExamples.explanation: The vertex of any quadratic ax²+bx+c gives the maximum (if a<0) or minimum (if a>0). For business profit modeled as quadratic, the vertex is where profit is maximized. Plugging neighboring points (x and x+1) verifies the maximum.

commonMistakes: Confusing f(g(x)) with f(x) × g(x) — composition is substitution, not multiplication.
Forgetting to check domain restrictions — if f(x) = 1/x, then f(g(x)) is undefined wherever g(x) = 0.
Assuming f(g(x)) = g(f(x)) — this is rarely true. Always compute both separately.

quiz.question: If f(x) = 2x + 1, what is f(3)?
quiz.explanation: f(3) = 2(3) + 1 = 7. Substitute 3 for x in the function definition.

quiz.question: What is the domain of f(x) = √(x - 4)?
quiz.explanation: The expression inside a square root must be non-negative: x - 4 ≥ 0, so x ≥ 4.

quiz.question: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = x + 1, what is f(g(2))?
What is the inverse of f(x) = 2x - 3?
quiz.explanation: Replace f(x) with y: y = 2x - 3. Swap x and y: x = 2y - 3. Solve for y: 2y = x + 3, so y = (x + 3)/2.

quiz.question: What is the domain of f(x) = 1/(x - 2)?
quiz.explanation: Division by zero is undefined, so x - 2 ≠ 0, meaning x ≠ 2. The domain is all real numbers except 2.

quiz.question: If f(x) = 2x + 3, g(x) = x², and h(x) = x - 1, what is f(g(h(2)))?
If f(x) = 3x - 6, what is f⁻¹(x)? Verify by checking f(f⁻¹(12)).

nodes.name: Limits & Continuity
nodes.description: The foundation of calculus. Understanding what happens as values approach infinity.
nodes.whyItMatters: Limits are the engine of calculus. Derivatives and integrals are both defined as limits. If you understand limits, you understand the soul of calculus.
practiceProblems: Evaluate: lim(x→2) (x² - 4)/(x - 2)
Where is f(x) = (x² - 1)/(x - 1) discontinuous?
Use squeeze theorem: find lim(x→0) x²·sin(1/x)

resources.title: Khan Academy: Limits
resources.whyHelpful: Step-by-step limit concepts from continuity to LHopitals rule. Interactive practice builds intuition.

resources.title: Essence of Calculus (3Blue1Brown)
resources.whyHelpful: The single best intuition-building resource for calculus. Watch this first before diving into computations.

resources.title: Limits Intuition (3Blue1Brown)
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of what a limit actually means. Essential foundation before derivatives.

resources.title: Wolfram Alpha: Limit Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Compute limits step-by-step. Use this to check your work and explore tricky limit problems.

workedExamples.problem: Evaluate: lim(x→3) (x² - 9)/(x - 3). Explain why direct substitution fails and how to solve it.
workedExamples.solution: Direct substitution gives 0/0, which is indeterminate. Factor: (x² - 9)/(x - 3) = (x+3)(x-3)/(x-3) = x + 3 (for x ≠ 3). So the limit = 3 + 3 = 6.
workedExamples.explanation: 0/0 is indeterminate, not zero. Factoring, rationalizing, or using L'Hôpital's rule can resolve these limits. The limit describes behavior near the point, not at the point.

workedExamples.problem: Determine if f(x) = (x² - 4)/(x - 2) is continuous at x = 2. If not, classify the discontinuity. Then evaluate lim(x→2) f(x).
workedExamples.solution: f(2) is undefined (division by zero), so f is not continuous at x = 2. However, lim(x→2) f(x) = lim(x→2) (x+2) = 4. Since the limit exists but f(2) is undefined, this is a removable discontinuity. We could redefine f(2) = 4 to make it continuous.
workedExamples.explanation: Continuity at x=a requires: (1) f(a) exists, (2) lim(x→a) f(x) exists, (3) they are equal. If the limit exists but f(a) doesn't, it's a removable discontinuity — the 'hole' can be filled by defining f(a) = limit.

workedExamples.problem: Evaluate lim(x→∞) (5x³ + 2x² - 3)/(2x³ - x + 7). What does this tell you about the end behavior of the function?
workedExamples.solution: Divide numerator and denominator by x³: lim = (5 + 2/x - 3/x³)/(2 - 1/x² + 7/x³) = 5/2. The limit is 5/2. This means the function has a horizontal asymptote at y = 5/2. As x grows very large, f(x) approaches 2.5.
workedExamples.explanation: For limits at infinity of rational functions, divide by the highest power of x. Terms with x in the denominator go to 0. If the degrees match, the limit is the ratio of leading coefficients. This is crucial for understanding long-term behavior in finance (compound interest converges) and algorithms (Big-O notation).

commonMistakes: Declaring 0/0 = 0 or 0/0 = undefined — it's indeterminate, meaning you need more work to find the actual limit.
Thinking the limit must equal the function value — limits can exist even when the function is undefined or has a different value at that point.
Forgetting to check left-hand and right-hand limits separately — they must agree for the limit to exist.

quiz.question: Evaluate: lim(x→2) (x² - 4)/(x - 2)
quiz.explanation: Factor: (x-2)(x+2)/(x-2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. So the limit = 2 + 2 = 4.

quiz.question: What does it mean for a function to be continuous at x = a?
options: The function has a value at a
The limit equals the function value, and both exist
The function is differentiable at a
quiz.explanation: Continuity requires three things: f(a) exists, lim(x→a) f(x) exists, and they are equal.

quiz.question: What is lim(x→0⁺) 1/x?
quiz.explanation: As x approaches 0 from the positive side, 1/x becomes larger and larger positive, so the limit is +∞.

quiz.question: A function f(x) has lim(x→3⁻) f(x) = 5 and lim(x→3⁺) f(x) = 5, but f(3) = 7. What type of discontinuity is at x = 3?
options: No discontinuity — the function is continuous
quiz.explanation: The left and right limits agree (both 5), so the limit exists. But f(3) = 7 ≠ 5, so there's a 'hole' at x=3 that could be 'filled' by redefining f(3) = 5. This is a removable discontinuity.

nodes.name: Integrals
nodes.description: Area under curves, accumulation, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
nodes.whyItMatters: Integrals accumulate. Total distance is the integral of velocity. Total profit is the integral of marginal profit. Probability distributions are integrated to find probabilities.
practiceProblems: ∫(3x² + 4x) dx
∫₀² x³ dx
∫ sin(x) dx
Find area between y = x² and y = x from x=0 to 1

resources.title: Khan Academy: Integrals
resources.whyHelpful: Complete integral calculus course from Riemann sums to advanced techniques. Structured practice problems.

resources.title: Essence of Calculus: Integration
resources.whyHelpful: Visual intuition for why integrals compute area and how they reverse derivatives. Essential viewing.

resources.title: Fundamental Theorem Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Clear explanation of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus -- the bridge between derivatives and integrals.

resources.title: Paul's Online Math Notes: Integrals
resources.whyHelpful: Reference sheet with integration formulas, techniques (substitution, parts), and fully worked examples.

workedExamples.problem: Evaluate ∫(4x³ + 6x) dx. Then compute the definite integral ∫₁² (2x + 3) dx and interpret the result.
workedExamples.solution: ∫(4x³ + 6x) dx = x⁴ + 3x² + C. Definite: ∫₁² (2x + 3) dx = [x² + 3x]₁² = (4 + 6) - (1 + 3) = 10 - 4 = 6. This is the net area between y = 2x + 3 and the x-axis from x=1 to x=2.
workedExamples.explanation: Indefinite integrals include +C (constant of integration). Definite integrals evaluate the antiderivative at the bounds and subtract. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration.

workedExamples.problem: Use u-substitution to evaluate ∫(2x · cos(x²)) dx. Then evaluate ∫₀^√π (2x · cos(x²)) dx.
workedExamples.solution: Let u = x², du = 2x dx. ∫cos(u) du = sin(u) + C = sin(x²) + C. Definite: sin((√π)²) - sin(0²) = sin(π) - sin(0) = 0 - 0 = 0. The area above and below the x-axis cancels out perfectly.
workedExamples.explanation: U-substitution reverses the chain rule. When the integrand contains a function and its derivative (or a multiple), substitution simplifies it. For definite integrals, you can either substitute back to x and use original bounds, or convert bounds to u-values.

workedExamples.problem: A factory's marginal revenue is MR(x) = 100 - 0.1x dollars per unit, where x is the number of units produced. Find the total revenue from producing 0 to 500 units. Then find the average revenue per unit over this range.
workedExamples.explanation: Integrating a rate of change (marginal revenue) gives the total accumulation. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in action. The average value of f(x) over [a,b] is (1/(b-a)) · ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. In economics, marginal cost/revenue curves are integrated to find total cost/revenue.

commonMistakes: Forgetting +C on indefinite integrals — the antiderivative is a family of functions, not a single function.
Subtracting bounds in the wrong order — ∫ₐᵇ = F(b) - F(a), not F(a) - F(b).
Applying the power rule incorrectly for integration — ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C, which is different from differentiation.

quiz.question: Evaluate ∫(3x²) dx
quiz.explanation: Using the power rule for integration: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. So ∫3x² dx = 3(x³/3) + C = x³ + C.

quiz.question: What does the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connect?
quiz.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus shows that differentiation and integration are essentially inverse operations, and it provides a way to evaluate definite integrals using antiderivatives.

quiz.question: Evaluate ∫₀² x dx.
quiz.explanation: Antiderivative of x is x²/2. Evaluate: (2²/2) - (0²/2) = 2 - 0 = 2.

quiz.question: What is ∫(2x · e^(x²)) dx?
quiz.explanation: Use u-substitution: let u = x², du = 2x dx. The integral becomes ∫e^u du = e^u + C = e^(x²) + C. The 2x is exactly the derivative of x², making this a clean substitution.

nodes.name: Matrices & Linear Algebra
nodes.description: Matrix operations, determinants, eigenvalues, and transformations. The math of AI and graphics.
nodes.whyItMatters: Linear algebra is the math behind AI, computer graphics, data compression, and quantum mechanics. Every neural network is matrices multiplying.
practiceProblems: Find determinant of [[2,3],[1,4]]
Find eigenvalues of [[2,1],[1,2]]
What transformation does [[0,-1],[1,0]] represent?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Linear Algebra
resources.whyHelpful: Comprehensive linear algebra with exercises on matrices, determinants, eigenvalues, and applications.

resources.title: Essence of Linear Algebra (3Blue1Brown)
resources.whyHelpful: The best intuitive introduction to linear algebra. Transformations as geometric operations, not just matrix arithmetic.

resources.title: MIT 18.06 Linear Algebra
resources.whyHelpful: Complete linear algebra course from MIT. Rigorous coverage of all core topics with real applications.

resources.title: Interactive Matrix Calculator
resources.whyHelpful: Visual matrix operations tool. Multiply, invert, and transform matrices while seeing the geometric effect.

workedExamples.problem: Multiply A = [[1, 2], [3, 4]] and B = [[5, 6], [7, 8]]. Find det(A). Find the eigenvalues of C = [[2, 1], [1, 2]].
workedExamples.explanation: Matrix multiplication is row-by-column. The determinant measures scaling factor and tells you if a matrix is invertible (det ≠ 0). Eigenvalues represent the scaling factors along the matrix's principal axes.

workedExamples.problem: Solve the system using matrices: 3x + 2y = 7, x - y = 1. Write it as Ax = b, then solve using A⁻¹. Verify by substituting back.
workedExamples.explanation: Any system of linear equations can be written as Ax = b. If A is invertible, the solution is x = A⁻¹b. This is how computers solve large systems — Gaussian elimination is the practical version of this. In data science, you solve (XᵀX)β = Xᵀy to find the best-fit line in linear regression.

workedExamples.problem: The matrix R = [[cos(θ), -sin(θ)], [sin(θ), cos(θ)]] represents a 2D rotation. Apply R with θ = 90° to vector v = (1, 0). Then find R² (applying rotation twice) and interpret the result.
workedExamples.solution: R(90°) = [[0, -1], [1, 0]]. R × v = [[0, -1], [1, 0]] × [[1], [0]] = [[0], [1]]. The vector (1,0) rotates to (0,1) — a 90° counterclockwise rotation. R² = [[0,-1],[1,0]] × [[0,-1],[1,0]] = [[-1,0],[0,-1]] = -I. This is a 180° rotation, which flips any vector to its negative.
workedExamples.explanation: Rotation matrices are fundamental in computer graphics and robotics. Composing rotations corresponds to matrix multiplication. R² = -I confirms that two 90° rotations equal one 180° rotation. This connects to complex numbers: i² = -1, and the rotation matrix is the real representation of multiplication by i.

workedExamples.problem: A portfolio has two assets with returns modeled by a covariance matrix Σ = [[0.04, 0.01], [0.01, 0.09]]. Find the eigenvalues of Σ. What do they tell you about portfolio risk? If you invest 60% in asset 1 and 40% in asset 2, what is the portfolio variance?
workedExamples.explanation: Covariance matrices in portfolio theory are symmetric and positive semi-definite. Their eigenvalues represent the variance explained by each principal component — this is the foundation of PCA (Principal Component Analysis) used in risk management. The portfolio variance calculation wᵀΣw shows how diversification reduces risk when assets are not perfectly correlated.

commonMistakes: Multiplying matrices element-wise — AB is not [aᵢⱼ × bᵢⱼ]. It's dot product of rows and columns.
Assuming AB = BA — matrix multiplication is not commutative in general.
Forgetting that a zero determinant means the matrix is singular (no inverse) — this indicates the transformation collapses space into a lower dimension.

quiz.question: What is the determinant of [[1, 2], [3, 4]]?
What does an eigenvalue represent?
options: A scalar that describes how much an eigenvector is stretched or shrunk by a transformation
quiz.explanation: An eigenvalue λ tells you that the transformation scales its corresponding eigenvector by factor λ, without changing its direction.

quiz.question: What is [[1,2],[3,4]] × [[5],[6]]?
quiz.explanation: Multiply rows by column: Row1: 1·5 + 2·6 = 17. Row2: 3·5 + 4·6 = 39. Result is [[17],[39]].

quiz.question: If a matrix has a determinant of 0, what does this mean?
options: The matrix is singular (no inverse exists)
The matrix is the identity matrix
quiz.explanation: A determinant of 0 means the matrix is singular — it collapses space into a lower dimension and has no inverse. This is critical in solving linear systems: det = 0 means either no solution or infinitely many solutions.

nodes.name: Rational Expressions
nodes.description: Simplify, add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational expressions. Master the algebra of fractions with polynomials.
nodes.whyItMatters: Rational expressions are used in rates, proportions, and calculus (limits, derivatives of rational functions). They model real-world scenarios like work rates and mixture problems.
practiceProblems: Simplify: (x²-4)/(x+2)
Solve: 2/(x-1) + 1/(x+2) = 1. What are the restrictions on x?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Rational Expressions
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's structured course on rational expressions with video lessons and practice problems. Covers simplifying, multiplying, dividing, adding, and subtracting rational expressions with clear explanations.

resources.title: Rational Expressions Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Video walkthrough of rational expression operations with step-by-step examples. Useful for visual learners who need to see the factoring and canceling process in action.

resources.title: MathIsFun: Rational Expressions
resources.whyHelpful: Accessible introduction to rational expressions with worked examples and practice problems. Great for reviewing factoring methods and understanding domain restrictions visually.

resources.title: Paul's Online Math Notes: Rational Expressions
resources.whyHelpful: Detailed lecture notes with fully worked examples covering simplifying, operations, and complex fractions. Excellent reference for step-by-step algebraic manipulation techniques.

quiz.question: Simplify: (x²-4)/(x+2)
quiz.explanation: Factor numerator: (x-2)(x+2). Cancel (x+2) for x ≠ -2: result is x-2.

quiz.question: What is the domain restriction for 1/(x-3)?
quiz.explanation: Denominator cannot be zero, so x-3 ≠ 0 → x ≠ 3.

quiz.question: Multiply: (x/2) * (4/x)

workedExamples.problem: Simplify (x²-9)/(x²-4x+3) * (x-1)/(x+3)
workedExamples.explanation: Always factor first, then cancel common factors. Remember domain restrictions: x ≠ 3, x ≠ 1, x ≠ -3.

workedExamples.problem: Add the rational expressions: 3/(x+2) + 2/(x-1) and simplify the result.
workedExamples.solution: Find common denominator: (x+2)(x-1). Rewrite: 3(x-1)/[(x+2)(x-1)] + 2(x+2)/[(x+2)(x-1)] = [3(x-1) + 2(x+2)]/[(x+2)(x-1)] = [3x-3+2x+4]/[(x+2)(x-1)] = (5x+1)/[(x+2)(x-1)]. The result cannot be simplified further since 5x+1 shares no common factor with x+2 or x-1.
workedExamples.explanation: To add rational expressions, find a common denominator by multiplying the distinct factors. Then combine numerators, distribute, and simplify. Even if the final answer looks messy, the key skill is the systematic approach: common denominator → rewrite → combine → simplify.

workedExamples.problem: Divide: (5x)/(x²-4) ÷ (3x)/(x+2) and simplify, listing all restrictions.
workedExamples.solution: Rewrite as multiplication: (5x)/[(x-2)(x+2)] * (x+2)/(3x). Cancel (x+2) and x: result = 5/[3(x-2)]. Restrictions: x ≠ 0, x ≠ 2, x ≠ -2.
workedExamples.explanation: Division of rational expressions means multiplying by the reciprocal. Always factor first, then cancel before multiplying. List all restrictions from both original expressions plus any cancellations.

commonMistakes: Forgetting domain restrictions after canceling factors
Adding denominators directly instead of finding common denominator
Canceling terms that are added, not multiplied (can't cancel x in (x+2)/x)

nodes.name: Exponential & Logarithmic Equations
nodes.description: Solve equations involving exponential and logarithmic functions. Model real-world growth and decay.
nodes.whyItMatters: Exponential equations model population growth, radioactive decay, compound interest, and spread of viruses. Logarithms help solve for time in these models.
practiceProblems: Solve: 2^x = 16
Solve: log₂(x) = 5
Solve: e^x = 10
How long to double $1000 at 5% annual interest?

resources.title: Khan Academy: Exponential & Log Equations
resources.whyHelpful: Khan Academy's structured course on exponential and logarithmic equations. Step-by-step practice problems with instant feedback help build confidence with solving for unknown exponents and logs.

resources.title: Exponential Growth Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of how exponential growth works in nature and finance. Builds intuition for why compound interest grows faster than linear growth over time.

resources.title: PurpleMath: Exponential & Log Equations
resources.whyHelpful: Clear, methodical walkthrough of solving exponential and logarithmic equations with worked examples. Great reference for understanding the 'why' behind each algebraic step.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Exponential Growth
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's elegant geometric explanation of exponential growth and the number e. Builds deep intuition for why exponential functions behave the way they do through animations and visual reasoning.

quiz.question: Solve: 3^x = 81
quiz.explanation: 81 = 3^4, so x=4. Alternatively, take log₃: x = log₃(81) = 4.

quiz.question: Solve: log₁₀(x) = 3
quiz.explanation: log₁₀(x)=3 means 10^3 = x → x=1000.

quiz.question: What is the half-life of a substance if 100g decays to 25g in 10 years?
quiz.explanation: 100g → 50g (1 half-life) → 25g (2 half-lives). 10 years = 2 half-lives, so 1 half-life = 5 years.

workedExamples.problem: Solve: 2^(x+1) = 32. Then solve: ln(x) + ln(x-2) = ln(3).
workedExamples.solution: 2^(x+1)=32=2^5 → x+1=5 → x=4. For log equation: ln(x(x-2))=ln(3) → x²-2x-3=0 → (x-3)(x+1)=0 → x=3 (x=-1 invalid, log domain).
workedExamples.explanation: For exponential equations, rewrite both sides with same base. For log equations, use log properties to combine, then exponentiate both sides. Always check domain.

workedExamples.problem: You invest $1000 at 8% annual interest compounded continuously. How long until it doubles? Then: An earthquake measured 6.0 on the Richter scale. A second earthquake measures 7.5. How many times more intense is the second earthquake?
workedExamples.solution:
Continuous compound: A = Pe^(rt). Doubled means 2000 = 1000·e^(0.08t) → 2 = e^(0.08t) → ln(2) = 0.08t → t = ln(2)/0.08 ≈ 0.693/0.08 ≈ 8.66 years. (Rule of 72: 72/8 = 9 years ≈ matches.)
Richter scale: Each unit = 10x intensity. Difference = 7.5 - 6.0 = 1.5. Ratio = 10^1.5 = 10^1 × 10^0.5 = 10 × 3.16 ≈ 31.6 times more intense.
workedExamples.explanation: The natural logarithm and e appear everywhere in growth/decay: continuously compounded interest, radioactive decay, population growth. The Rule of 72 (72/rate ≈ doubling time in years) is a handy approximation derived from ln(2) ≈ 0.693. Logarithmic scales (Richter, decibels) compress enormous ranges into manageable numbers — a 1.5 difference on a log scale means 31.6x difference in the actual quantity.

workedExamples.problem: A company loses 25% of its employees each year. If it starts with 400 employees, how many remain after 3 years (rounded down)? Also: amino acids have pKa=2.34, pH=8.2. What fraction is protonated?
workedExamples.solution: Exponential decay: N = N_0 * (1 - r)^t = 400 * (0.75)^3 = 400 * 0.422 = 168.8 ≈ 168 employees. For Henderson-Hasselbalch: fraction protonated = 1 / (1 + 10^(pH-pKa)) = 1 / (1 + 10^(5.86)) ≈ 1 / (1 + 7.24e5) ≈ 1/724001 ≈ 1.38e-6, effectively 0%.
workedExamples.explanation: Workforce attrition with constant annual rate is exponential decay. Keep fractional people until the final rounding step. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation extends to the exact fraction protonated via acid dissociation: fraction protonated = 1 / [1 + 10^(pH-pKa)]. When pH >> pKa over 5 units above, the deprotonated form dominates almost completely.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to check log domain (x>0)
Using ln instead of log base 10 incorrectly
Not rewriting exponential equations with same base before solving

nodes.name: Probability Basics
nodes.description: Fundamental probability concepts: events, sample spaces, conditional probability, and Bayes' theorem.
nodes.whyItMatters: Probability is the language of uncertainty. Used in finance (risk models), AI (Naive Bayes), medicine (test accuracy), and everyday decision-making.
practiceProblems: P(heads) in fair coin flip
P(rolling 6 on die) given first roll was 3
Expected value of dice roll

resources.title: Khan Academy: Probability
resources.whyHelpful: Advanced probability covering conditional probability, Bayes theorem, random variables, and distributions.

resources.title: Bayes' Theorem Explained
resources.whyHelpful: 3Blue1Brown's elegant visual explanation of Bayes' theorem. Makes the formula intuitive through geometry and interactive reasoning rather than abstract algebra.

resources.title: Stat Trek: Probability Rules
resources.whyHelpful: Concise reference for basic probability rules, addition/multiplication laws, and conditional probability. Includes practice problems with clear step-by-step solutions.

resources.title: Brilliant: Expected Value
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive explanation of expected value with real-world applications in games, finance, and decision theory. Includes challenging problems to test understanding.

quiz.question: What is P(rolling 3 on a fair 6-sided die)?
quiz.explanation: 6 equally likely outcomes, 1 favorable: P=1/6.

quiz.question: If P(A)=0.6, P(B)=0.5, P(A∩B)=0.3, what is P(A|B)?
What is the expected value of rolling a fair 6-sided die?

workedExamples.problem: A test for a disease is 99% accurate (P(positive|disease)=0.99, P(negative|no disease)=0.99). Disease prevalence is 1%. What is P(disease|positive)?
workedExamples.solution: Use Bayes: P(D|+)=P(+|D)P(D)/[P(+|D)P(D)+P(+|no D)P(no D)] = 0.99*0.01 / (0.99*0.01 + 0.01*0.99) = 0.5. Only 50% chance of having disease even with positive test!
workedExamples.explanation: Low prevalence means most positives are false positives. This is why screening tests for rare diseases need high specificity.

workedExamples.problem: Two events: A (P=0.3) and B (P=0.4) are independent. What is P(A and B)? What is P(A or B)? Explain why independent events are NOT the same as mutually exclusive.
workedExamples.solution: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B) = 0.3 * 0.4 = 0.12. P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) = 0.3 + 0.4 - 0.12 = 0.58. Mutually exclusive events cannot happen together (P(A AND B) = 0), so if mutually exclusive then P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) without subtracting. Independent events CAN happen together, so subtract the overlap.
workedExamples.explanation: Independence means P(A|B) = P(A) — B occurring does not change the probability of A. Multiplication rule for independent events is simple: P(A and B) = P(A)*P(B). Addition rule P(A or B) always subtracts P(A and B) to avoid double-counting the overlap. Mutually exclusive is the special case where P(A and B) = 0 — the events cannot both happen. Independence is much harder to achieve than mutual exclusivity; most real events are neither fully independent nor mutually exclusive.

workedExamples.problem: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. What is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads? What is the expected number of heads in 3 flips? Explain the difference between the probability of a specific outcome and expected value.
workedExamples.solution: Exactly 2 heads: There are C(3,2)=3 ways to get 2H1T (HHT, HTH, THH). Each has probability 0.5^3=1/8. Total P(2H) = 3/8 = 0.375. Expected number of heads: E = n*p = 3*0.5 = 1.5 heads. Probability tells you how likely a specific outcome is. Expected value is the average result across many repetitions — even though you can never flip 1.5 heads in 3 flips, over 1000 trials you'd expect ~500 heads.
workedExamples.explanation: Binomial distribution: P(X=k) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k). Expected value E[X]=n*p is the theoretical long-run average. A single trial yields a concrete outcome, but E[X] lets you reason about what happens across thousands of repetitions without running them.

commonMistakes: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A)
Forgetting that probabilities must be between 0 and 1
Not considering base rates in Bayes' theorem
Assuming a positive test result means infection is certain — base rate matters more when the disease is rare

nodes.name: Trigonometry Basics
nodes.description: SOH CAH TOA, unit circle, and basic trig identities. Master the sine, cosine, and tangent.
nodes.whyItMatters: Trigonometry is essential for waves, signals, navigation, and game programming. Every sine wave is a rotating circle.
practiceProblems: Find cos(60°)
Unit circle coordinates at 45°
Solve for θ: sinθ = 0.5

resources.title: Khan Academy: Trigonometry
resources.whyHelpful: Advanced trig covering identities, inverse functions, and modeling periodic phenomena.

resources.title: Essence of Trigonometry
resources.whyHelpful: Visual breakdown of sine, cosine, and tangent as circular functions. Builds genuine geometric intuition.

resources.title: Unit Circle Interactive
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive unit circle showing how sine and cosine correspond to coordinates as the angle changes.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Sine Waves Explained
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of sine waves and their relationship to circular motion and oscillation.

quiz.question: What is sin(30°)?
quiz.explanation: In 30-60-90 triangle, opposite side is half hypotenuse, so sin30=1/2.

quiz.question: Which ratio is tangent?
quiz.explanation: TOA: Tangent = Opposite / Adjacent.

quiz.question: What is cos(45°)?
quiz.explanation: In 45-45-90 triangle, legs are 1, hypotenuse √2. cos45=1/√2=√2/2.

workedExamples.problem: Find sin(45°), cos(45°), and tan(45°) without a calculator.
workedExamples.explanation: Memorize special triangles: 30-60-90 (1, √3, 2) and 45-45-90 (1,1,√2).

workedExamples.problem: A price chart shows a cyclic pattern that can be modeled as P(t) = 50 + 10·sin(πt/6) where t is the month number (t=0 is January). What is the price in March (t=2)? What is the maximum price and when does it first occur?
workedExamples.solution: March (t=2): P(2) = 50 + 10·sin(π×2/6) = 50 + 10·sin(π/3) = 50 + 10×(√3/2) ≈ 50 + 8.66 = $58.66. Maximum price: sin reaches 1 when πt/6 = π/2 → t = 3 (April). Max price = 50 + 10 = $60. Period: 2π/(π/6) = 12 months.
workedExamples.explanation: Sine waves model cyclical patterns in markets, seasons, and signals. The amplitude (10) is the deviation from the midline (50). The period tells you how long one complete cycle takes. Understanding trig functions helps identify cyclical patterns in price data.

workedExamples.problem: A satellite is orbiting Earth at an altitude where its ground track forms an angle θ with the equator. If the satellite is 400 km above Earth (radius ≈ 6,371 km), and the orbital inclination is 51.6° (like the ISS), how far north of the equator does the satellite reach at its maximum?
workedExamples.solution: Maximum latitude = orbital inclination = 51.6°. Distance north along Earth's surface: d = r × θ where θ is in radians. θ = 51.6° × π/180 ≈ 0.9006 rad. d = 6371 × 0.9006 ≈ 5,738 km. The satellite reaches approximately 5,738 km north of the equator. To find the straight-line height above the equator (not surface distance): the vertical component = (6371 + 400) × sin(51.6°) ≈ 6771 × 0.7837 ≈ 5,306 km.
workedExamples.explanation: Trigonometry connects angles to distances in orbital mechanics, navigation, and physics. The sine of an angle gives you the vertical component of any vector. This same math applies to calculating antenna angles for satellite dishes, the height of buildings, and resolving forces in engineering.

commonMistakes: Confusing sine and cosine — sine is opposite/hyp, cosine is adjacent/hyp.
Using degrees when calculator is in radian mode — always check calculator mode.
Forgetting inverse trig has restricted domains — arcsin only outputs -90° to 90°.

nodes.name: Calculus II
nodes.description: Advanced integration techniques, sequences and series, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. The next step after Calculus I.
nodes.whyItMatters: Calculus II is essential for engineering, physics, and advanced mathematics. Integration techniques are used in probability, statistics, and machine learning. Taylor series let you approximate any function with polynomials.
practiceProblems: ∫ x·sin(x) dx using integration by parts
∫ 1/(x²-1) dx using partial fractions
Determine if the series Σ 1/n² converges
Find the Taylor series for e^x centered at 0

resources.title: Khan Academy: Calculus II
resources.whyHelpful: Advanced calculus covering integration techniques, sequences, series, and polar coordinates.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Taylor Series
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of approximating functions with polynomials. Makes Taylor series intuitively clear.

resources.title: MIT 18.01 Calculus II
resources.whyHelpful: Rigorous second-semester calculus from MIT. Covers integration, series, and differential equations.

resources.title: Paul's Online Math Notes: Integration Techniques
resources.whyHelpful: Comprehensive reference covering integration by parts, trig substitution, partial fractions, and improper integrals. Excellent worked examples for each technique with step-by-step solutions.

quiz.question: What is ∫ x·e^x dx using integration by parts?
quiz.explanation: Let u = x, dv = e^x dx. Then du = dx, v = e^x. ∫x·e^x dx = x·e^x - ∫e^x dx = x·e^x - e^x + C.

quiz.question: Which test determines if Σ 1/n² converges?
quiz.explanation: Σ 1/n^p converges when p > 1. Here p=2, so the series converges.

quiz.question: What is the Taylor series for e^x centered at 0?

workedExamples.problem: Evaluate ∫ x·cos(x) dx using integration by parts. Then find the first 3 terms of the Taylor series for sin(x) centered at 0.
workedExamples.solution: For ∫x·cos(x)dx: let u=x, dv=cos(x)dx → du=dx, v=sin(x). Result: x·sin(x) - ∫sin(x)dx = x·sin(x) + cos(x) + C. Taylor series for sin(x): sin(x) = x - x³/3! + x⁵/5! - ... So first 3 terms: x - x³/6 + x⁵/120.
workedExamples.explanation: Integration by parts formula: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du. Taylor series for sin(x) is an alternating series with odd powers only.

workedExamples.problem: Use partial fractions to evaluate ∫(3x + 5)/(x² - 1) dx. Then determine if the improper integral ∫₁^∞ 1/x² dx converges or diverges.
workedExamples.solution: Factor denominator: x²-1 = (x-1)(x+1). (3x+5)/[(x-1)(x+1)] = A/(x-1) + B/(x+1). 3x+5 = A(x+1) + B(x-1). Set x=1: 8=2A → A=4. Set x=-1: 2=-2B → B=-1. ∫[4/(x-1) - 1/(x+1)]dx = 4·ln|x-1| - ln|x+1| + C. Improper integral: ∫₁^∞ 1/x² dx = lim[b→∞] [-1/x]₁^b = lim[b→∞](-1/b + 1) = 1. Converges to 1.
workedExamples.explanation: Partial fractions break complex rational functions into simpler terms. For improper integrals, replace the infinite bound with a variable and take the limit. The p-test tells us ∫₁^∞ 1/x^p dx converges when p > 1.

workedExamples.problem: A probability density function is f(x) = cx² for 0 ≤ x ≤ 3, and 0 elsewhere. (a) Find the constant c that makes this a valid PDF. (b) Calculate the expected value E[X]. (c) What is P(1 < X < 2)?
workedExamples.solution:
(a) A PDF must integrate to 1: ∫₀³ cx² dx = c[x³/3]₀³ = c(27/3) = 9c = 1 → c = 1/9.
(b) E[X] = ∫₀³ x·f(x)dx = ∫₀³ x·(x²/9)dx = (1/9)∫₀³ x³dx = (1/9)[x⁴/4]₀³ = (1/9)(81/4) = 81/36 = 2.25.
(c) P(1 < X < 2) = ∫₁² (x²/9)dx = (1/9)[x³/3]₁² = (1/27)(8 - 1) = 7/27 ≈ 0.259.
So the expected value of this distribution is 2.25, and there's a 25.9% chance X falls between 1 and 2.
workedExamples.explanation: Integration is the backbone of probability theory. PDFs must have total area = 1. Expected value is the first moment — the integral of x·f(x). This connects calculus directly to statistics: every probability distribution, from normal to exponential, requires integration to compute its properties. In data science and ML, these integrals appear in likelihood functions, Bayesian inference, and information theory.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the +C on indefinite integrals — all antiderivatives need the constant of integration.
Choosing wrong u and dv in integration by parts — LIATE rule helps (Log, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential).
Confusing convergence tests — p-series test only works for Σ 1/n^p, not all series.

nodes.name: Graph Theory & Networks
nodes.description: Model relationships, connections, and flows with graphs. From friendship networks to the internet to neural networks — graphs power our understanding of how things connect, how fast information spreads, and what the shortest path really is.
keyConcepts: Directed vs undirected graphs: one-way streets vs two-way
Weighted graphs: edges have costs or distances
Adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations
BFS and DFS: the two fundamental graph traversal algorithms
Dijkstra's algorithm: shortest paths in weighted graphs
Minimum spanning trees: connecting everything at minimum cost (Kruskal, Prim)
Network flow: max flow / min cut theorem
PageRank: graphs define importance on the web
nodes.whyItMatters: Graph theory is the language of connection. Your social media feed, Google Maps directions, the internet routing packets, your neural network's computation graph — they're all graphs. PageRank, the algorithm that made Google, is a graph algorithm. Recommendation systems use graph-based collaborative filtering. Understanding graph algorithms means understanding how everything from your brain to the internet processes connectivity.
practiceProblems: A social network has 1,000 users and 50,000 friendship edges. How would you find all friends within 2 degrees of a given user (friends of friends)? Which traversal algorithm and why?
A delivery driver needs to visit 10 cities with known distances between each pair. What type of problem is this, and is Dijkstra's algorithm the right tool?
A graph has 100 nodes and the adjacency matrix is 100x100 = 10,000 entries. How many entries would you need to check for BFS vs DFS on a sparse graph (avg 5 edges/node)?
Explain how PageRank uses graph structure to determine that a page with few but high-quality inbound links can rank higher than a page with many low-quality links.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Graph Theory Introduction
resources.whyHelpful: Visually intuitive introduction to graphs as abstractions of connection. Covers basic definitions, Eulerian paths, and why graph theory matters.

resources.title: Computerphile: Dijkstra's Algorithm
resources.whyHelpful: Clear step-by-step walkthrough of Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm. Shows the priority queue in action and explains why it finds optimal routes.

resources.title: StatQuest: PageRank
resources.whyHelpful: Explains Google's PageRank algorithm as a graph problem — how random surfers, damping factors, and eigenvector centrality combine to rank web pages.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Graph Theory
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive course on graph algorithms with visualizations of BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, and A* search. Hands-on coding exercises reinforce understanding.

resources.title: Visualgo: Graph Data Structures
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive visualization of graph representations (adjacency matrix vs list), traversals, and shortest path algorithms. Toggle between algorithms and watch them step through graphs.

quiz.question: A graph with no cycles is called:
quiz.explanation: An acyclic graph — one with no cycles — is called a tree. Trees are the simplest connected graphs and form the backbone of data structures like binary search trees, heaps, and spanning trees.

quiz.question: Dijkstra's algorithm finds:
options: The shortest path between two nodes in a weighted graph
The maximum flow in a network
quiz.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It's the algorithm behind Google Maps routing.

quiz.question: For a graph with V vertices and E edges, the time complexity of BFS using an adjacency list is:
quiz.explanation: BFS visits each vertex once (O(V)) and checks each edge once (O(E)), so total time is O(V + E). For an adjacency matrix it would be O(V²) because you'd need to check every possible edge for each vertex.

workedExamples.problem: You have 5 cities with flight connections: A→B (2h), A→C (5h), B→C (1h), B→D (4h), C→D (2h), C→E (3h), D→E (1h). Find the shortest path from A to E using Dijkstra's algorithm.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Initialize distances: A=0, B=∞, C=∞, D=∞, E=∞. Visit A. Step 2: From A, update B=2, C=5. Visit B (shortest unvisited). Step 3: From B, update C=min(5, 2+1=3)=3, D=2+4=6. Visit C. Step 4: From C, update D=min(6, 3+2=5)=5, E=3+3=6. Visit D. Step 5: From D, update E=min(6, 5+1=6)=6. Visit E. Answer: Shortest path A→B→C→E at 6 hours, or A→B→D→E also 6 hours (two equal paths). Dijkstra confirms both are optimal because the distance is the same.
workedExamples.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm greedily visits the closest unvisited node, updating neighbor distances. It works because once a node's distance is finalized (visited), all remaining paths to it are longer (non-negative edges). This guarantees optimality and runs in O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue. Google Maps uses a variant of Dijkstra that incorporates traffic data as dynamic edge weights.

workedExamples.problem: A social network has 1 million users. You want to find all users within 3 degrees of a target user (friends, friends-of-friends, friends-of-friends-of-friends). The average user has 150 friends. Estimate how many users you'd need to visit using BFS. Which representation (adjacency matrix vs list) would be more efficient?
workedExamples.solution: BFS from the target: Level 1: ~150 friends. Level 2: ~150 × 150 = 22,500 (some overlap, but let's estimate ~20,000). Level 3: ~20,000 × 150 = 3,000,000 (but overlap with already-visited is significant; realistically ~300,000 new unique users). Total visited: ~322,150 users. For representation: adjacency list (stores ~150 entries per user × 1M = 150M entries ~ 1.2GB) is far more efficient than adjacency matrix (1M × 1M = 10^12 entries ~ 4TB). BFS on adjacency list is O(V+E) = O(322K + 48M edges) ~ manageable. The '6 degrees of separation' phenomenon means most users are reachable within 6 hops in a social network.
workedExamples.explanation: This is why social networks use adjacency lists — the average degree (density) is low. An adjacency matrix wastes quadratic space for a sparse graph. BFS in this context is also called 'friend-of-friend search' and is the core algorithm behind 'People You May Know' recommendations. The branching factor (150 friends) means the frontier grows exponentially, but real-world networks have high overlap (mutual friends) that limits the explosion.

workedExamples.problem: A streaming service has 10 server nodes connected in a network. The max data flow between each server pair (in Gbps) forms a weighted directed graph. The source server S can send data at 50 Gbps total, and the destination server D can receive at 50 Gbps. Using the max-flow min-cut theorem, how would you find the bottleneck in the network?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Model the network as a flow network with directed edges and capacities. Step 2: Use the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm to find the maximum flow from S to D. Step 3: The max flow value equals the capacity of the minimum cut — the smallest set of edges that, if removed, disconnect S from D. Step 4: The algorithm repeatedly finds augmenting paths (unsaturated paths from S to D) and pushes flow along them. Step 5: The edges in the minimum cut are the network bottleneck — they represent the weakest links limiting throughput. For example, if max flow is 30 Gbps but S can send 50 Gbps, the bottleneck edges (min cut) cost 20 Gbps of lost capacity.
workedExamples.explanation: The max-flow min-cut theorem is a cornerstone result: the maximum amount of flow that can be sent from source to sink equals the capacity of the minimum cut. This is used in network design (finding bottlenecks), image segmentation (cutting graphs), and even Match.com's dating algorithms (maximum bipartite matching). It's also deeply connected to circuit analysis and transportation planning.

commonMistakes: Confusing BFS (queue-based, good for shortest path in unweighted graphs) with DFS (stack-based, good for connectivity, cycle detection, topological sort). BFS finds shortest paths, DFS finds if a path exists.
Forgetting Dijkstra doesn't work with negative edge weights — use Bellman-Ford instead. Dijkstra assumes that once a node is visited, its distance is final, which breaks with negative edges.
Thinking adjacency matrices are better for large sparse graphs — they're O(V²) memory which is prohibitive for a million-node social network. Use adjacency lists for sparse graphs.
Using DFS when you need shortest path — DFS explores deep branches first and might find a very long path before the short one. BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.

nodes.name: Complex Numbers
nodes.description: Move beyond the real number line into the complex plane. Complex numbers are the backbone of quantum mechanics, signal processing, control theory, and modern physics. They turn hard trig problems into simple algebra.
keyConcepts: Imaginary unit i: i² = -1 and why it matters
Complex plane: real vs imaginary axes
Cartesian form: z = a + bi and operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide)
Complex conjugate: z̄ = a - bi and its geometric meaning
Polar form: z = r(cos θ + i sin θ)
Euler's formula: e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ
De Moivre's theorem: (cos θ + i sin θ)^n = cos(nθ) + i sin(nθ)
Roots of unity: solving z^n = 1 in the complex plane
nodes.whyItMatters: Complex numbers aren't 'imaginary' — they're a second dimension of numbers. Every AC circuit, radio signal, quantum wavefunction, and Fourier transform runs on complex arithmetic. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), which powers everything from JPEG compression to WiFi, is complex arithmetic. Neural network activations that use phase (complex-valued networks) can represent 2× more information per neuron. Mastering complex numbers opens the door to advanced math, physics, and signal processing.
practiceProblems: Simplify: (3+2i) + (5-7i). Then multiply: (3+2i)(5-7i). Express both answers in a+bi form.
Find the modulus and argument of z = 1 + i. Express z in polar form.
Use Euler's formula to express e^(iπ) + 1 in simplest form. What famous identity is this?
Find all 4th roots of unity (solutions to z⁴ = 1). Plot them on the complex plane — what shape do they form?

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Complex Numbers Fundamentals
resources.whyHelpful: Visual deep dive into the complex plane — why multiplying by i rotates by 90°, the geometric meaning of complex arithmetic, and Euler's formula made intuitive.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Complex Numbers
resources.whyHelpful: Structured from basic operations through polar form and complex plane visualization. Interactive exercises with instant feedback.

resources.title: BetterExplained: A Visual Guide to Complex Numbers
resources.whyHelpful: Explains why 'imaginary' numbers are actually about 2D rotation and dimension. Best conceptual introduction you'll find.

resources.title: Brilliant: Complex Numbers Interactive
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive visualizations of complex plane, Euler's formula, and roots of unity. Strong prerequisites for advanced topics.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Euler's Formula with Differential Equations
resources.whyHelpful: Beautiful proof that Euler's formula e^(iπ) = -1 emerges naturally from differential equations. Essential intuition for why e and π and i belong together.

resources.title: BetterExplained: Intuitive Understanding of Euler's Formula
resources.whyHelpful: Companion article to BetterExplained's complex numbers guide. Makes e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ feel natural instead of magical.

quiz.question: What is (3+2i) + (5-7i) expressed in a+bi form?
quiz.explanation: Add real parts: 3+5=8. Add imaginary parts: 2i + (-7i) = -5i. Result: 8-5i.

quiz.question: What is the product (3+2i)(5-7i)?
What is Euler's formula?
options: e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ
e^(iθ) = i sin θ + cos θ
quiz.explanation: Euler's formula is e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ. Both options B and C say the same thing (addition is commutative: cos θ + i sin θ = i sin θ + cos θ). Option A (e^(iπ) = -1) is Euler's identity, a special case of the formula when θ = π.

quiz.question: What geometric operation does multiplying by i correspond to on the complex plane?
quiz.explanation: Multiplying by i rotates a point 90° counterclockwise around the origin. i·(a+bi) = ai + bi² = ai - b = -b + ai. Check: point (1,0) rotated becomes (0,1). ✓

quiz.question: Find the modulus |z| of z = 3 + 4i
quiz.explanation: |z| = √(a² + b²) = √(3² + 4²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5. The modulus is the distance from the origin to the point (a,b) on the complex plane.

workedExamples.problem: Simplify (3+2i)/(1-i). Express in a+bi form.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of denominator: (3+2i)/(1-i) × (1+i)/(1+i). Step 2: Numerator: (3+2i)(1+i) = 3(1) + 3(i) + 2i(1) + 2i(i) = 3 + 3i + 2i + 2i² = 3 + 5i - 2 = 1 + 5i. Step 3: Denominator: (1-i)(1+i) = 1(1) + 1(i) - i(1) - i(i) = 1 + i - i - i² = 1 + 0 - (-1) = 2. Step 4: (1+5i)/2 = 1/2 + (5/2)i. Final: 0.5 + 2.5i.
workedExamples.explanation: To divide complex numbers, multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator. The conjugate makes the denominator real because (a+bi)(a-bi) = a² + b². This is analogous to rationalizing denominators with square roots — the conjugate eliminates the imaginary part.

workedExamples.problem: Express z = 1 + i in polar form, then use De Moivre's theorem to compute z⁶.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Modulus: |z| = √(1²+1²) = √2. Step 2: Argument: θ = arctan(1/1) = π/4. So z = √2(cos π/4 + i sin π/4). Step 3: By De Moivre: z⁶ = (√2)⁶(cos(6·π/4) + i sin(6·π/4)) = 8(cos(3π/2) + i sin(3π/2)) = 8(0 + i(-1)) = -8i.
workedExamples.explanation: Polar form multiplies distances (moduli) and adds angles (arguments). De Moivre extends this to powers: you raise the modulus to the power and multiply the angle. z⁶ rotates z by 6 × 45° = 270° to land on the negative imaginary axis.

workedExamples.problem: Find all 4th roots of unity (solve z⁴ = 1) and plot them. Describe the shape they form.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Write 1 in polar form: 1 = cos(0) + i sin(0) = cos(2πk) + i sin(2πk) for k = 0,1,2,3. Step 2: By De Moivre, the 4th roots are: z_k = cos(2πk/4) + i sin(2πk/4). Step 3: Compute each: z₀ = cos(0)+i sin(0) = 1. z₁ = cos(π/2)+i sin(π/2) = i. z₂ = cos(π)+i sin(π) = -1. z₃ = cos(3π/2)+i sin(3π/2) = -i. These form a square on the unit circle at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°.
workedExamples.explanation: Roots of unity are evenly spaced around the unit circle. For zⁿ = 1, the n roots form a regular n-gon. This pattern is fundamental to the Discrete Fourier Transform — the DFT matrix is built from roots of unity. In signal processing, these roots define the frequency 'bins' that decompose a signal into its frequency components.

commonMistakes: Forgetting i² = -1 when multiplying complex numbers — every time you see i², replace it with -1, never leave it as-is.
Confusing complex conjugate with negation — the conjugate of a+bi is a-bi (flip sign of imaginary part), NOT -a-bi (which would be negation).
Trying to compare complex numbers with inequality signs — there's no 'greater than' on the complex plane. |z₁| > |z₂| compares magnitudes, not the numbers themselves.

Using the wrong formula for argument: arctan(b/a) only works in the first quadrant. For (a,b) in quadrants II or III, add π to arctan(b/a). Most calculators and programming languages have atan2(b,a) that handles this automatically.

nodes.name: Mathematical Proofs & Reasoning
nodes.description: Proof is the backbone of mathematics — it separates opinion from certainty. Learn to construct rigorous arguments: direct proofs, proof by contradiction, induction, and contrapositives. These reasoning patterns are the same ones that power algorithm correctness proofs, program verification, and formal logic in AI.
keyConcepts: Axioms, theorems, lemmas, and corollaries — the hierarchy of mathematical truth
Direct proof: assume P, derive Q using logical steps
Proof by contradiction: assume ¬Q, derive contradiction with known truths
Proof by contrapositive: prove "if not Q then not P" instead of "if P then Q"
Mathematical induction: base case + inductive step → holds for all natural numbers
Strong induction: assume true for ALL smaller values, not just n-1
Existence proofs: constructive (build it) vs non-constructive (show it must exist)
Proof by cases: exhaustively handle every possible scenario
Counterexamples: one counterexample disproves a universal claim
Logical connectives: AND, OR, NOT, IMPLIES, IFF and their truth tables
nodes.whyItMatters: Proofs aren't just for mathematicians. Every time you write an 'if this then that' statement in code, you're reasoning like a proof. When you prove an algorithm terminates, that's induction. When you debug by assuming the opposite and finding a contradiction, that's proof by contradiction. In AI safety, formal proofs verify that models satisfy constraints. In cryptography, security guarantees are mathematical proofs. Learning to prove teaches you to think clearly, communicate precisely, and argue convincingly — skills that transfer to every field.
practiceProblems: Prove by direct proof: if n is an even integer, then n² is also even.
Prove by contradiction: √2 is irrational (hint: assume √2 = a/b in lowest terms).
Prove by induction: 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 for all natural numbers n.
Suppose a conjecture claims "every odd number > 1 is prime." Find a counterexample. Is the original statement true or false?
Prove the contrapositive: If n² is odd, then n is odd. (Hint: prove "if n is even then n² is even" instead.)

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Lockdown Math — The Essence of Proof
resources.whyHelpful: Accessible introduction to what proofs are and why they matter, with visual examples of induction and contradiction that build deep intuition.

resources.title: Brilliant: Mathematical Proof Course
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive course covering direct proof, contradiction, induction, and cases. Each concept has practice problems with instant feedback.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Induction Proofs
resources.whyHelpful: Focused induction track with dozens of worked examples from simple summation to complex inequality proofs.

resources.title: Book of Proof — Richard Hammack (Free PDF)
resources.whyHelpful: Full textbook available free online. Chapters on logic, sets, direct proof, contradiction, induction, and more. The best free resource for learning proofs.

resources.title: BetterExplained: Understanding Proof by Contradiction
resources.whyHelpful: Intuitive explanation of why contradiction works, with historical examples like Euclid's proof of infinite primes. Great conceptual companion.

resources.title: Gödel, Escher, Bach — Douglas Hofstadter
resources.whyHelpful: The classic exploration of formal systems, proofs, self-reference, and incompleteness. Not a textbook, but the deepest cultural context for what proofs mean.

quiz.question: Prove directly: If n is even, which of the following MUST be true about n²?
quiz.explanation: If n is even, n = 2k for some integer k. Then n² = (2k)² = 4k² = 2(2k²). Since 2(2k²) is 2 times an integer, n² is even. Direct proof: assume the hypothesis, substitute the definition, derive the conclusion.

quiz.question: What does proof by contradiction assume to start the argument?
options: Both hypothesis and conclusion are false
quiz.explanation: Proof by contradiction assumes the NEGATION of what you want to prove (conclusion is false), and then shows this leads to a contradiction with known facts. Since the contradiction is impossible, the original claim must be true.

quiz.question: In proof by induction, after proving the base case P(1), what do you prove in the inductive step?
options: P(k) is true for all k
If P(k) is true then P(k+1) is true
P(k+1) is true regardless of P(k)
P(k) is false for some k
quiz.explanation: The inductive step proves the implication: P(k) ⇒ P(k+1). Together with the base case P(1), this forms a chain: P(1) is true, so P(2) is true, so P(3) is true... like dominoes falling. This covers all natural numbers.

quiz.question: What proves a universal statement like 'all swans are white' is FALSE?
options: One example of a black swan
Showing it's true for most swans
quiz.explanation: A single counterexample disproves a universal claim. This is why scientific theories can never be proven (confirmed), only disproven (falsified). One black swan overturns 'all swans are white' forever, no matter how many white swans you've seen.

quiz.question: Write the contrapositive of 'If it rains, the ground gets wet.'
options: If the ground is wet, it rained
If it doesn't rain, the ground stays dry
If the ground is not wet, it did not rain
If it rains, the ground stays dry
quiz.explanation: The contrapositive of 'if P then Q' is 'if not Q then not P'. Here, P='it rains', Q='ground wet'. Not Q = 'ground not wet', Not P = 'it did not rain'. So contrapositive: 'If the ground is not wet, it did not rain.' This is logically equivalent to the original statement — proving one proves the other.

workedExamples.problem: Prove that √2 is irrational using proof by contradiction.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Assume the opposite — that √2 IS rational. Step 2: Then √2 = a/b where a,b are integers with no common factors (fraction in lowest terms). Step 3: Square both sides: 2 = a²/b², so a² = 2b². Step 4: This means a² is even, so a is even. Write a = 2k. Step 5: Substitute: (2k)² = 2b², so 4k² = 2b², so 2k² = b². Step 6: This means b² is even, so b is even. Step 7: But if a AND b are both even, they share a factor of 2 — contradicting our assumption that a/b is in lowest terms. Step 8: Our assumption must be false, so √2 is irrational.
workedExamples.explanation: This is the classic contradiction proof. We assumed the negation (√2 is rational) and derived two contradictory facts: a/b is in lowest terms AND a and b share a factor of 2. The contradiction forces the original claim to be true. This proof is over 2,000 years old (Euclid) and is considered one of the most elegant in all of mathematics.

workedExamples.problem: Prove by induction that 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 for all natural numbers n.
workedExamples.solution: Base case (n=1): LHS = 1. RHS = 1(1+1)/2 = 2/2 = 1. ✓. Inductive step: Assume true for n = k: 1+2+...+k = k(k+1)/2. Prove for n = k+1: 1+2+...+k+(k+1) = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k(k+1) + 2(k+1))/2 = (k+1)(k+2)/2 = (k+1)((k+1)+1)/2. ✓. By induction, proven for all n ∈ ℕ.
workedExamples.explanation: Induction works like dominoes. We proved P(1) is true (first domino falls). Then we proved that if P(k) is true, P(k+1) follows (each domino knocks over the next). Therefore P(n) is true for EVERY n. This summation formula is attributed to Gauss at age 7 — he noticed 1+100=101, 2+99=101, ... 50+51=101, so 50×101 = 5050. This very proof you just did is the formal version of his insight.

workedExamples.problem: Prove that for any integer n, n² + n is always even (divisible by 2). Use proof by cases.
workedExamples.solution: Case 1: n is even. Then n = 2k. So n² + n = (2k)² + 2k = 4k² + 2k = 2(2k² + k). This is 2 times an integer, so it's even. ✓. Case 2: n is odd. Then n = 2k+1. So n² + n = (2k+1)² + (2k+1) = (4k²+4k+1) + (2k+1) = 4k²+6k+2 = 2(2k²+3k+1). This is 2 times an integer, so it's even. ✓. Since both cases (even and odd) cover all possible integers, n² + n is always even.
workedExamples.explanation: Proof by cases exhaustively examines every possibility. Since every integer is either even or odd, showing the claim holds in both cases proves it for all integers. This is more elegant than induction here — and notice we could also solve it: n²+n = n(n+1), which is always the product of two consecutive integers, and one of them must be even.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'proof by contradiction' with 'proof by contrapositive' — contradiction assumes ¬conclusion and derives ANY contradiction. Contrapositive proves 'if not Q then not P' as a direct replacement for 'if P then Q'. They are different tools.

Forgetting the base case in induction — without P(1), the inductive chain has no starting point. Proving P(k) ⇒ P(k+1) without P(1) is like proving 'each domino knocks over the next' but never pushing the first domino.

Thinking one example 'proves' a universal statement — no matter how many examples you find, a universal claim needs a proof, not evidence. One counterexample is enough to disprove, but a million examples don't prove.

Assuming what you want to prove — circular reasoning (begging the question) is the most common logical error in beginner proofs. You must start from axioms or already-proven theorems, not from the statement itself.
Using induction on non-integer or non-well-ordered sets — induction only works on sets with a well-ordering (like natural numbers). You can't directly induct on real numbers.

nodes.name: Set Theory
nodes.description: Set theory is the foundation of all modern mathematics. Everything — from numbers to functions to infinity — can be built from sets. Learn the language of sets, relations between them, cardinality, and the counterintuitive nature of infinite sets. This is the grammar of mathematical thought.
keyConcepts: Set definition: a well-defined collection of distinct objects (elements)
Set notation: roster {1,2,3}, set-builder {x | P(x)}, and Venn diagrams
Subsets, proper subsets, and the empty set ∅
Set operations: union ∪, intersection ∩, difference , complement '
Power set P(S): the set of all subsets of S
Cartesian product A × B: ordered pairs (a,b)
Cardinality: finite vs countably infinite vs uncountably infinite
Cantor's diagonal argument: uncountability of real numbers
nodes.whyItMatters: Set theory is the universal language of mathematics. When you write code, every type system, every collection (arrays, sets, maps), every iterator is built on set-theoretic concepts. The SQL JOIN is a Cartesian product. The type 'boolean' is a set of two values. Understanding cardinality changes how you think about infinity itself — there are different sizes of infinity, and that matters for computability theory (some problems are too big for any computer to solve, ever). In probability, sample spaces are sets. In data science, features are sets. In databases, relations are sets. You cannot escape sets.
practiceProblems: Let A = {1,2,3} and B = {3,4,5}. Compute A ∪ B, A ∩ B, A  B, and B  A.
List the power set P({a,b,c}). How many subsets does a set with n elements have? Prove it.
Define f: ℤ → ℤ by f(x) = 2x. Is f injective? Surjective? Bijective? Prove your answer.
Show that the set of even integers has the same cardinality as the set of all integers (ℤ).
Prove that the interval (0,1) is uncountable using Cantor's diagonal argument.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Counting — A Meditation on Infinity
resources.whyHelpful: Visual deep dive into different sizes of infinity — countably vs uncountably infinite — with Cantor's diagonal argument made intuitive through animated geometry.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Set Theory Basics
resources.whyHelpful: Fundamentals of set operations with Venn diagram visualizations and practice exercises.

resources.title: Brilliant: Set Theory Foundations
resources.whyHelpful: Comprehensive interactive guide from basic set operations through cardinality. Excellent for building intuition on infinite sets.

resources.title: Stanford Encyclopedia: Set Theory
resources.whyHelpful: Authoritative introduction covering ZFC axioms, Russell's paradox, and the foundations of mathematics. Deep but rewarding.

resources.title: Computerphile: Cantor's Diagonal Argument
resources.whyHelpful: Clear explanation of why there are more real numbers than integers, with implications for computing and undecidability.

resources.title: Set Theory for Computer Science — Stanford CS
resources.whyHelpful: Stanford's CS103 notes on set theory applied to automata, computability, and formal languages. Connects math to CS.

quiz.question: If A = {1,2,3,4} and B = {3,4,5,6}, what is A ∩ B?
quiz.explanation: A ∩ B is the intersection — elements common to both sets. Only 3 and 4 appear in both A and B.

quiz.question: How many elements are in the power set of a set with 4 elements?
quiz.explanation: The power set P(S) contains all subsets. A set with n elements has 2^n subsets (each element is either in or out). For n=4: 2^4 = 16.

quiz.question: A function f: X → Y is bijective if and only if it is:
quiz.explanation: A bijection is both injective (each input maps to a unique output) and surjective (every output is reached by some input). This creates a perfect pairing — a one-to-one correspondence — between elements of X and Y.

quiz.question: Which of the following sets has the LARGEST cardinality?
quiz.explanation: ℕ, ℤ, and ℚ are all countably infinite (same size). ℝ is uncountably infinite — strictly larger. Cantor's diagonal argument proves you cannot list all real numbers, no matter how clever your listing.

quiz.question: What is the cardinality of the Cartesian product A × B if |A| = 3 and |B| = 4?
quiz.explanation: |A × B| = |A| × |B| = 3 × 4 = 12 ordered pairs. Each of the 3 elements from A pairs with each of the 4 elements from B.

workedExamples.problem: Prove that the set of even integers E = {2k | k ∈ ℤ} has the same cardinality as ℤ.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Define a function f: ℤ → E by f(k) = 2k. Step 2: f is injective: If f(k₁)=f(k₂), then 2k₁=2k₂, so k₁=k₂. Step 3: f is surjective: For any even integer e ∈ E, e=2k for some k, and f(k)=e. Step 4: Since f is both injective and surjective, it's a bijection, so |E| = |ℤ|.
workedExamples.explanation: This shows that a proper subset (the even integers) can have the same size as the whole set (all integers). This is impossible for finite sets but is the defining property of infinite sets. In fact, a set is infinite if and only if it has a bijection to a proper subset of itself.

workedExamples.problem: Use Cantor's diagonal argument to prove that the real numbers between 0 and 1 are uncountable.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Assume (for contradiction) that the numbers in (0,1) are countable. Then we can list them: r₁, r₂, r₃, ... Step 2: Write each r_n in decimal form: r₁ = 0.d₁₁d₁₂d₁₃..., r₂ = 0.d₂₁d₂₂d₂₃..., etc. Step 3: Construct a new number s = 0.s₁s₂s₃... where s_n = 5 if d_nn ≠ 5, and s_n = 6 if d_nn = 5. Step 4: s differs from r₁ in the first decimal place, from r₂ in the second, etc. So s is not in the list. Step 5: But s is a real number in (0,1), contradicting our assumption that we listed all of them. Therefore (0,1) is uncountable.
workedExamples.explanation: Cantor's diagonal argument is one of the most revolutionary proofs in mathematics. It showed for the first time that there are different sizes of infinity — a discovery that shocked mathematicians and led to entire new branches of set theory and logic. The key insight: no matter how cleverly you try to enumerate the reals, you can always construct one that evades the list by flipping the diagonal digits.

workedExamples.problem: Prove De Morgan's Law: (A ∪ B)' = A' ∩ B' using set membership arguments.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1: Show (A ∪ B)' ⊆ A' ∩ B'. Take any x ∈ (A ∪ B)'. Then x ∉ (A ∪ B). So x ∉ A AND x ∉ B. Thus x ∈ A' AND x ∈ B', so x ∈ A' ∩ B'. Step 2: Show A' ∩ B' ⊆ (A ∪ B)'. Take any x ∈ A' ∩ B'. Then x ∈ A' AND x ∈ B', so x ∉ A AND x ∉ B. Thus x ∉ (A ∪ B), meaning x ∈ (A ∪ B)'. Step 3: Since both directions hold, (A ∪ B)' = A' ∩ B'. ✓
workedExamples.explanation: De Morgan's Laws connect unions and complements. They're essential for simplifying logical expressions AND/OR/NOT in both math and programming (they work the same in Boolean algebra and digital logic: NOT(A OR B) = NOT(A) AND NOT(B)). The proof technique here — showing two-way subset inclusion — is the standard method for proving set equalities.

commonMistakes: Confusing ∈ (element of) with ⊆ (subset of) — {1} ∈ {1,2} is false (the set {1} is not an element), but {1} ⊆ {1,2} is true ({1} is a subset).
Thinking the empty set ∅ has zero subsets — it has one: ∅ itself. P(∅) = {∅}.
Forgetting that a function must map EVERY input — a partial function (mapping only some elements) is not a function.
Confusing codomain with range (image) — the range is a subset of the codomain. A function is surjective if its range equals its codomain.

Believing 'infinity is infinity' — Cantor proved there are infinitely many sizes of infinity (ℵ₀, ℵ₁, ℵ₂, ...). The real numbers are strictly larger than the integers, and power sets generate ever-larger infinities.

nodes.name: Real Analysis Basics
nodes.description: Real analysis puts calculus on a rigorous foundation. Instead of hand-waving about 'infinitesimally small' changes, analysis defines limits, continuity, and convergence with epsilon-delta precision. This is where math stops being about computation and becomes about proof and structure. Master the real number system itself.
keyConcepts: Real numbers as a complete ordered field: the least upper bound (supremum) property
Sequences: definition, convergence, limits of sequences
Epsilon-N definition of sequence convergence: ∀ε>0 ∃N such that n>N ⇒ |aₙ-L|<ε
Monotone sequences and the monotone convergence theorem
Subsequences and the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem (every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence)
Cauchy sequences: a sequence where terms get arbitrarily close to each other
Completeness of ℝ: every Cauchy sequence converges in ℝ
Limits of functions: epsilon-delta definition of continuity
Intermediate Value Theorem: continuous functions on intervals take all intermediate values
Series: convergence tests (comparison, ratio, root, integral test)
nodes.whyItMatters: Real analysis is the microscope that reveals why calculus works. Why can you differentiate a polynomial term-by-term? Why does the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantee a root exists? Why does an infinite series either converge or diverge? Analysis answers these questions. For computer science, analysis underpins numerical methods — understanding floating-point error, convergence of iterative algorithms (gradient descent, Newton's method), and when approximations are valid. In machine learning, the proof that gradient descent converges relies on analysis. In physics, the wave equation solutions depend on analysis. If you want to truly understand calculus — not just compute with it — you need analysis.
practiceProblems: Prove that the sequence aₙ = 1/n converges to 0 using the epsilon-N definition.
Determine whether the series Σ 1/n² converges or diverges. What about Σ 1/n? Prove your answer.
Prove using epsilon-delta that f(x) = x² is continuous at x = 2.
Show that the sequence aₙ = (-1)ⁿ does NOT converge (prove it's not Cauchy).
Use the Intermediate Value Theorem to prove that x³ - x - 1 = 0 has a root in [1,2].

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Limits and the Epsilon-Delta Definition
resources.whyHelpful: Animated visual explanation of the epsilon-delta definition — why 'as close as you want, as long as you get close enough' is the precise language of limits.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Sequences and Series
resources.whyHelpful: Structured path from sequences through convergence tests with practice problems. Good foundation before diving into real analysis proofs.

resources.title: Brilliant: Real Analysis Foundations
resources.whyHelpful: Interactive exploration of limits, continuity, and convergence with visual feedback. Bridges computational calculus and rigorous analysis.

resources.title: Understanding Analysis — Stephen Abbott (Free Chapters)
resources.whyHelpful: The most readable real analysis textbook. Abbott explains epsilon-delta proofs with such clarity that the subject becomes approachable. Widely considered the best intro analysis text.

resources.title: Terence Tao: Analysis I (Free Online)
resources.whyHelpful: Fields Medalist's rigorous but accessible intro to analysis. Covers natural numbers through sequences, series, and continuity. Built from the axioms up.

resources.title: MIT OCW: Real Analysis
resources.whyHelpful: Complete MIT course with lecture videos, problem sets, and exams. Starts from axioms of ℝ and builds up to differentiation and integration.

quiz.question: What does it mean for a sequence aₙ to converge to a limit L?
options: The terms get closer to L
For every ε > 0, there exists N such that |aₙ - L| < ε for all n > N
quiz.explanation: The epsilon-N definition: for ANY positive epsilon (no matter how small), there is a point N in the sequence after which ALL terms are within epsilon of L. This captures 'eventually arbitrarily close.'

quiz.question: Which of the following sequences is NOT Cauchy?
quiz.explanation: A Cauchy sequence has terms that get arbitrarily close to each other. aₙ = (-1)ⁿ alternates between 1 and -1 — consecutive terms are always 2 apart, never getting close. Since ℝ is complete, a sequence converges iff it is Cauchy.

quiz.question: The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem states:
options: Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence
quiz.explanation: Bolzano-Weierstrass: every bounded sequence (in ℝ) has a convergent subsequence. This is a powerful existence theorem — even if a sequence doesn't converge, if it's bounded you can extract a convergent subsequence.

quiz.question: The series Σ 1/n (harmonic series) diverges, while Σ 1/n² converges. Which test proves this?
quiz.explanation: The integral test shows Σ 1/nᵖ converges if p > 1 and diverges if p ≤ 1. Since ∫₁^∞ (1/x) dx diverges (ln x → ∞), the harmonic series diverges. But ∫₁^∞ (1/x²) dx = 1, so Σ 1/n² converges to π²/6 (Basel problem).

quiz.question: What does the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantee?
options: Every continuous function has a maximum
If f is continuous on [a,b] and y is between f(a) and f(b), then f(c)=y for some c in [a,b]
